questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose a metallic resistor is connected across a battery. If the number of collisions of the free electrons with the lattice in the resistor is somehow reduced (for instance, by cooling it), the current will
questions.solution:
The drift velocity of free electrons in a metallic conductor is given by $v_d = \dfrac{eE}{m}\tau$, where $\tau$ is the relaxation time (the average time between successive collisions).

If the number of collisions of free electrons with the lattice is reduced, the relaxation time $\tau$ increases.

As a result, the drift velocity $v_d$ of the electrons increases.

The electric current flowing through the resistor is given by $I = neAv_d$.

Since the drift velocity $v_d$ increases, the current $I$ will increase.

Alternatively, the resistance of the conductor is $R = \dfrac{ml}{ne^2\tau A}$. An increase in $\tau$ leads to a decrease in resistance $R$. For a constant potential difference $V$ provided by the battery, the current $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ will increase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two resistors, labeled $A$ and $B$, have resistances $R_A$ and $R_B$ respectively, where $R_A < R_B$. The materials of these resistors have resistivities $ho_A$ and $ho_B$.
options: The provided information is insufficient to establish a relation between $\rho_A$ and $\rho_B$.
questions.solution:
The resistance of a conductor is given by $R = \rho \dfrac{l}{A}$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity of the material, $l$ is the length, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

Given $R_A < R_B$, we have $\rho_A \dfrac{l_A}{A_A} < \rho_B \dfrac{l_B}{A_B}$

Since the dimensions ($l$ and $A$) of the two resistors are not provided, we cannot compare the resistivities $\rho_A$ and $\rho_B$.

The provided information is insufficient to establish a relation between $\rho_A$ and $\rho_B$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electric circuit, two resistances $R$ and $2R$ are wired in parallel. The ratio of the thermal energy developed in $R$ to that in $2R$ is
questions.solution:
When two resistances are connected in parallel, the potential difference $V$ across them is the same.

The thermal energy developed in a resistance $R$ in time $t$ is given by $H = \dfrac{V^2}{R} t$

For the first resistance $R_1 = R$, the thermal energy is $H_1 = \dfrac{V^2}{R} t$

For the second resistance $R_2 = 2R$, the thermal energy is $H_2 = \dfrac{V^2}{2R} t$

The ratio of the thermal energy developed in $R$ to that in $2R$ is

$\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V^2}{R} t}{\dfrac{V^2}{2R} t} = \dfrac{2R}{R} = 2$

Thus, the ratio is $2 : 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform wire with a resistance of $50 	ext{ }\Omega$ is cut into $5$ equal parts. These parts are subsequently connected in parallel. Determine the equivalent resistance of the combination.
questions.solution:
The resistance of the uniform wire is $R = 50 \Omega$.

When the wire is cut into $5$ equal parts, the resistance of each part becomes $r = \dfrac{R}{5} = \dfrac{50}{5} = 10 \Omega$.

When these $5$ parts are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$ is given by:

$\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{r} + \dfrac{1}{r} + \dfrac{1}{r} + \dfrac{1}{r} + \dfrac{1}{r} = \dfrac{5}{r}$

$R_{eq} = \dfrac{r}{5} = \dfrac{10}{5} = 2 \Omega$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two nonideal batteries are connected in series. Evaluate the following statements:
(A) The equivalent emf is greater than either of the two emfs.
(B) The equivalent internal resistance is less than either of the two internal resistances.
options: Both A and B are true
B is true but A is incorrect
A is true but B is incorrect
Both A and B are incorrect
questions.solution:
When two nonideal batteries of emfs $E_1$ and $E_2$ and internal resistances $r_1$ and $r_2$ are connected in series, the equivalent internal resistance is given by $r_{eq} = r_1 + r_2$.

Since $r_1 > 0$ and $r_2 > 0$, the equivalent internal resistance $r_{eq}$ is strictly greater than both $r_1$ and $r_2$. Therefore, statement B is incorrect.

The equivalent emf of the series combination depends on the polarity of the connection. If the batteries are connected such that they aid each other, $E_{eq} = E_1 + E_2$. However, if they are connected with opposing polarities, $E_{eq} = |E_1 - E_2|$. In the opposing case, the equivalent emf is less than the larger of the two individual emfs. Therefore, statement A is not universally true and is incorrect.

Thus, both statements A and B are incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two nonideal batteries are wired in parallel. Evaluate the following statements:
(A) The equivalent emf is smaller than either of the individual emfs.
(B) The equivalent internal resistance is smaller than either of the individual internal resistances.
options: A is correct but B is incorrect
B is correct but A is incorrect
Both A and B are correct
Both A and B are incorrect
questions.solution:
For two batteries connected in parallel with emfs $E_1$ and $E_2$ and internal resistances $r_1$ and $r_2$, the equivalent emf is given by $E_{eq} = \dfrac{E_1 r_2 + E_2 r_1}{r_1 + r_2}$.

This expression shows that $E_{eq}$ is a weighted average of $E_1$ and $E_2$. Thus, $E_{eq}$ always lies between $E_1$ and $E_2$ and cannot be smaller than both individual emfs. Hence, statement A is incorrect.

The equivalent internal resistance is given by the parallel combination of the individual internal resistances, $r_{eq} = \dfrac{r_1 r_2}{r_1 + r_2}$.

In a parallel combination of resistors, the equivalent resistance is always strictly less than the smallest individual resistance. Hence, statement B is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ammeter must have a small net resistance to ensure that
options: it does not significantly alter the current to be measured
it is capable of measuring large currents
questions.solution:
An ammeter is always connected in series with the circuit to measure the current flowing through it.

If the ammeter has a significant resistance, it will increase the equivalent resistance of the entire circuit.

According to Ohm's law, $I = \dfrac{V}{R_{eq}}$, an increase in the equivalent resistance will decrease the current in the circuit.

To ensure that the insertion of the ammeter does not significantly alter the original current to be measured, its net resistance must be kept very small (ideally zero).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electric circuit, two resistors of resistance $R$ and $2R$ are connected in series. The ratio of the thermal energy developed in $R$ to that in $2R$ is
questions.solution:
In a series circuit, the current $I$ flowing through all the resistors is the same.

The thermal energy developed in a resistor of resistance $R$ over a time $t$ is given by Joule's law of heating:

$H = I^2 R t$

For the first resistor of resistance $R$, the thermal energy developed is:

$H_1 = I^2 R t$

For the second resistor of resistance $2R$, the thermal energy developed is:

$H_2 = I^2 (2R) t$

The ratio of the thermal energy developed in $R$ to that in $2R$ is:

$\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \dfrac{I^2 R t}{I^2 (2R) t} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Thus, the ratio is $1 : 2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Within a battery connected in an electric circuit, the charge (assumed to be positive)
options: always moves from the positive terminal to the negative terminal
may move from the positive terminal to the negative terminal
always moves from the negative terminal to the positive terminal
questions.solution:
When a battery is discharging in a circuit, it acts as a source of electrical energy. In this case, positive charge moves from the negative terminal to the positive terminal inside the battery due to internal chemical forces.

When the battery is being charged by an external source, the direction of current is reversed. In this case, positive charge moves from the positive terminal to the negative terminal inside the battery.

Thus, within a battery, positive charge may move from the positive terminal to the negative terminal depending on whether it is charging or discharging.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the temperature of a metallic resistor is raised, the product of its resistivity and conductivity
questions.solution:
Resistivity $\rho$ and conductivity $\sigma$ are reciprocals of each other.

$\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$

The product of resistivity and conductivity is $\rho \sigma = \rho \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho}\right) = 1$.

Since the product is always equal to $1$, it is independent of temperature. Thus, when the temperature of a metallic resistor is raised, the product of its resistivity and conductivity remains constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a current flowing through a resistor. Let $K_1$ and $K_2$ denote the average kinetic energies of the conduction electrons and the metal ions, respectively. Which of the following is true?
options: Any of these three may occur
questions.solution:
When a current flows through a resistor, the applied electric field continuously accelerates the conduction electrons, thereby increasing their kinetic energy.

These energetic electrons undergo frequent collisions with the metal ions in the lattice, transferring a portion of their kinetic energy to the ions. This energy transfer is responsible for the Joule heating of the resistor.

For a continuous net transfer of energy from the conduction electrons to the metal ions, the average kinetic energy of the conduction electrons ($K_1$) must be greater than the average kinetic energy of the metal ions ($K_2$).

Furthermore, according to the quantum free electron model, the average kinetic energy of conduction electrons is determined by the Fermi energy, which is typically of the order of a few electron-volts (eV). In contrast, the average kinetic energy of the metal ions is determined by the thermal energy ($\_approx \dfrac{3}{2}k_BT$), which is only about $0.04$ eV at room temperature.

Therefore, $K_1 > K_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A voltmeter's net resistance should be large in order to ensure that
options: it does not appreciably alter the potential difference being measured
it can measure high potential differences
questions.solution:
A voltmeter is connected in parallel across the component whose potential difference is to be measured.

If the resistance of the voltmeter is not large, it will draw a significant amount of current from the main circuit. This reduces the current flowing through the component, thereby altering the potential difference across it.

To ensure that the voltmeter does not appreciably alter the potential difference being measured, its net resistance must be very large (ideally infinite).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Analyze the two statements provided below:
(A) Kirchhoff's junction law is based on the conservation of charge.
(B) Kirchhoff's loop law is based on the conservative nature of the electric field.
options: Both A and B are true
B is true but A is false
A is true but B is false
Both A and B are false
questions.solution:
Kirchhoff's junction law states that the algebraic sum of currents at any junction in a circuit is zero. This implies that charge does not accumulate at any junction, which is a direct consequence of the law of conservation of charge. Thus, statement (A) is true.

Kirchhoff's loop law states that the algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop is zero. This is based on the law of conservation of energy. The potential difference is the work done per unit charge by the electric field. Since the electrostatic field is conservative in nature, the total work done in moving a charge around a closed loop is zero. Thus, statement (B) is also true.

Both statements (A) and (B) are true.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a capacitor-charging circuit, let $Q_1$ denote the charge delivered to the capacitor during an initial time interval of $10 \text{ ms}$, and let $Q_2$ denote the charge delivered during the subsequent $10 \text{ ms}$ interval. Additionally, assume that an initial $10 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ of charge is deposited over a time interval $t_1$, while the next $10 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ of charge is deposited over the following time interval $t_2$.
questions.solution:
The charge on a capacitor during charging is given by $q(t) = Q_0(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.

The charging current is the rate of flow of charge, given by $i(t) = \dfrac{dq}{dt} = \dfrac{Q_0}{RC} e^{-t/RC}$.

Since the current $i(t)$ decreases exponentially with time, the rate at which charge is delivered to the capacitor continuously decreases.

For a fixed time interval, the charge delivered is the area under the $i(t)$ curve. Because $i(t)$ is a decreasing function, the charge delivered in the first $10 \text{ ms}$ interval is greater than the charge delivered in the subsequent $10 \text{ ms}$ interval. Thus, $Q_1 > Q_2$.

Similarly, to deposit a fixed amount of charge ($10 \mu\text{C}$), it takes less time initially when the current is high, and more time later when the current is lower. Therefore, the time $t_1$ required to deposit the first $10 \mu\text{C}$ is less than the time $t_2$ required to deposit the next $10 \mu\text{C}$. Thus, $t_1 < t_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a cylindrical conductor, the product of its resistivity and conductivity depends upon
options: no physical parameters, being identically equal to $1$
questions.solution:
Resistivity is denoted by $\rho$ and conductivity is denoted by $\sigma$.

By definition, conductivity is the reciprocal of resistivity, i.e., $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$.

The product of resistivity and conductivity is $\rho \times \sigma = \rho \times \dfrac{1}{\rho} = 1$.

Since the product is a dimensionless constant equal to $1$, it does not depend on any physical parameters such as material, cross-sectional area, or temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal battery is connected across a resistor of resistance $R$. If the value of $R$ is decreased, the power dissipated in the resistor will
questions.solution:
For an ideal battery, the potential difference $V$ across the resistor remains constant and is equal to the electromotive force of the battery.

The power dissipated in the resistor is given by $P = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$.

Since $V$ is constant, $P \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$.

Therefore, when the resistance $R$ is decreased, the power dissipated $P$ will increase.
